seoTitle: How to Insert Math Equations in Google Docs (2026)
metaDescription: Write fractions, square roots, and Greek symbols in Google Docs using the built-in equation editor, without pasting text from other tools.
intro: Typing fractions, integrals, or Greek variables by hand in Google Docs usually leads to broken formatting. This guide shows how to use the built-in equation editor to build clean mathematical and scientific notation directly in your document.

steps.heading: Open the equation editor
steps.body: Click Insert in the top menu and select Equation to open the equation toolbar above your document.

steps.heading: Start a new equation box
steps.body: Click 'New Equation' to open a fresh formula container where you can begin typing.

steps.heading: Build a fraction
steps.body: Choose the fraction preset from the Operations dropdown, type your numerator, press Tab, then type your denominator.

steps.heading: Insert symbols and operators
steps.body: Use the Greek letters, relations, and math operators menus to add variables like alpha or beta, and symbols like square roots.

steps.heading: Resize your equation
steps.body: Highlight the finished formula and adjust the font size so it matches the scale of your surrounding text.

faq.question: Can I type equations using LaTeX syntax?
faq.answer: Google Docs supports some LaTeX-style shortcuts inside the equation editor for faster symbol entry.

faq.question: Will my equations display correctly if shared with someone else?
faq.answer: Yes, equations render the same way for anyone viewing the document, regardless of their device.

faq.question: Can I copy an equation to another document?
faq.answer: Yes, you can copy and paste an equation box between Google Docs files.

faq.question: Is there a keyboard shortcut for common symbols?
faq.answer: Typing certain character sequences inside the equation box can auto-convert them into symbols like fractions or roots.
